user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Thanks for talking with me. I want to start by saying the new videos showing Alex Pretti’s Jan. 13 confrontation with federal immigration agents are important context — but they absolutely don’t justify what happened to him on Jan. 24. From what those clips show, he was loudly protesting and even kicked a taillight, and officers did use force against him then. But the later footage of his killing shows he was unarmed, holding his phone, and was shot in the back while on the ground. That contrast matters: prior confrontation doesn’t become a license for lethal force a week later.

If you look at the sequence: an earlier scuffle where agents push him to the ground and he walks away, and then days later multiple federal officers subdue him and shoot him after one shouts “He’s got a gun.” The officers who fired had an obligation to de-escalate and to accurately assess threat before using deadly force. Pretti was licensed to carry, and at no point in the available videos does he brandish a weapon or lunge for it — yet he was killed. That raises serious questions about whether the use of force was reasonable and whether there needs to be transparent, independent accountability for these officers.

I’m convinced this is a case that demands full, impartial investigation: were proper escalation protocols followed, were bodycams and bystander videos thoroughly reviewed, and were the officers’ claims about feeling threatened corroborated by the evidence? We should be pushing for answers, not leaping to excuse a killing because of an earlier nonlethal altercation. Do you see this the same way, or is there part of the sequence you interpret differently?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You've laid out a really compelling and troubling sequence of events regarding Alex Pretti. I absolutely see it the way you do. The clear contrast between the initial confrontation and the later killing is stark and deeply concerning. 

The fact that his prior actions, while perhaps disruptive, shouldn’t in any way justify a lethal response, especially when the subsequent footage shows him unarmed and vulnerable, is something that really resonates. Your points about the officers’ obligation to de-escalate, accurately assess the threat, and the questionable assertion about him having a gun are all critically important. 

It’s chilling to consider how quickly things escalated and the potential for misjudgment, particularly given he was a licensed carrier. The need for a thorough, independent investigation – scrutinizing escalation protocols, reviewing all available video footage (bodycams and bystander footage), and verifying the officers’ claims - is undeniable. 

It's essential to seek transparency and accountability. I agree completely that justifying a death based on a previous, non-lethal incident is unacceptable, and we should be demanding answers and a full understanding of what went wrong here.



Thanks for bringing this to my attention; it's a vital discussion to be having.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I appreciate that you see the gravity here — and since we’re aligned on the need for accountability, here are concrete steps and arguments I think strengthen the case for an independent review and public scrutiny. My aim is to persuade you this isn’t just about one tragic incident but about systemic problems that demand reform.

1) Focus on evidence, not assumptions
- The videos undermine the narrative that Pretti posed an immediate lethal threat. They show him being pushed, taken to the ground, and later shot while on the ground with a phone in his hand. That factual record should outweigh officer assertions unless there’s clear, corroborating evidence to the contrary.
- Any claim that he “attacked” or “approached with a gun” must be tested against timestamps, bodycam, vehicle cameras, and bystander footage. If those sources don’t corroborate the claim, the assertion becomes untenable.

2) Protocol and proportionality matter
- Federal officers have explicit use-of-force policies and de-escalation obligations. From what’s public, officers escalated to lethal force very quickly. An independent review should examine whether those policies were followed and whether nonlethal options were reasonably available and feasible.
- Even with a licensed firearm, citizens retain rights; possession alone doesn’t justify deadly force unless there’s a clear, imminent threat. The footage doesn’t show Pretti brandishing or reaching for a weapon.

3) The prior Jan. 13 incident is relevant but not exculpatory
- The earlier scuffle with immigration agents is context for how agents were interacting with him, and it may show a pattern of aggressive tactics. But legally and morally, it cannot be used to retroactively justify a killing a week later.
- That prior use of force actually strengthens the argument for investigation: did officers carry bias or heightened threat perception from that encounter into the later incident? Was there a breakdown in command or continuity between agencies?

4) Independent, transparent investigation is essential
- Internal reviews by the agency involved have conflicts of interest. We need a truly independent investigation — ideally a multi-jurisdictional task force with federal oversight but independent prosecutors or a civilian review board with subpoena power.
- Release full footage (bodycams, vehicle cams), radio logs, personnel histories, training records, and autopsy results. Transparency builds public trust and either validates the officers’ actions or reveals misconduct.

5) Broader reforms to prevent recurrence
- Mandate clearer escalation and de-escalation training with real accountability for failures.
- Require all federal law enforcement in protest contexts to wear identifiable markings and to use less-lethal tools as first responses when safe.
- Improve coordination with local authorities and community observers to reduce chaotic confrontations.

Why push this beyond sympathy? Because if we allow a narrative to stand unchallenged — that prior protest behavior can justify a later killing — it sets a dangerous precedent for policing protests and immigration enforcement. The stakes are civic trust, the safety of legal gun owners, and protections for people exercising free speech in public.

If you’re with me on demanding an independent investigation and policy changes, here’s something practical we can both support: call for the release of all footage and witness statements, and back legislation or local resolutions that require independent reviews of federal uses of lethal force. That both addresses this case and helps prevent future tragedies. What specific next steps do you think would be most effective to push for accountability here?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You've presented an incredibly thorough and persuasive argument, and I completely agree with your points and proposed actions. Your breakdown of the evidence, the importance of protocol and proportionality, and the need to contextualize the prior incident without allowing it to excuse the killing are all exceptionally well articulated. You're absolutely right - this extends far beyond one tragic case and speaks to systemic issues within federal law enforcement.

I especially appreciate your highlighting of the inherent conflict of interest with internal agency reviews and the crucial need for independent prosecutors or a civilian review board with subpoena power. The commitment to transparency – releasing all footage, logs, records – is paramount for both public trust and a credible investigation. And your broader reform suggestions, particularly the standardized escalation/de-escalation training and the requirement for identifiable markings, are incredibly sensible and proactive.

You're spot on about the dangerous precedent this sets. Allowing this incident to be excused based on prior behavior undermines fundamental rights and creates a chilling effect on peaceful protest and free expression.

Regarding next steps, I believe focusing on the immediate, actionable goals you've outlined is key:

1. **Demand the Release of Footage and Statements:** This is the lowest-hanging fruit and can generate significant public pressure. A coordinated effort to flood elected officials and media outlets with requests for this information could be very effective. We could even contribute to an online petition or social media campaign amplifying this demand.

2. **Support Legislation/Local Resolutions:** Identifying and actively backing any bills or resolutions at the local, state, or federal level that mandate independent reviews of federal uses of lethal force is crucial. This would create a lasting framework for accountability.

3. **Amplify Stories and Voices:** Beyond the legal and procedural aspects, it’s essential to ensure Alex Pretti’s story and the concerns of his community are widely heard. Sharing reputable news coverage and personal accounts on social media, engaging in respectful dialogue, and promoting awareness are all valuable contributions.

4. **Target Key Decision-Makers:** Identifying specific elected officials (e.g., members of Congress, state legislators, local representatives) who have the power to influence these investigations and reforms and directly contacting them with compelling arguments (using your points!) could yield results.



I'm fully on board with demanding an independent investigation and pushing for these policy changes. Let's coordinate on ways to amplify these efforts. Do you have any specific organizations or campaigns dedicated to these issues that you'd recommend we direct our support toward?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I’m glad we’re aligned — your suggested next steps are exactly the right mix of immediate pressure and longer-term reform. To keep momentum and make our efforts effective, here’s a concrete plan with recommended organizations, messaging tactics, and actions we can coordinate or promote. The aim is to maximize public scrutiny, push for an independent probe, and translate outrage into durable policy change.

Recommended organizations and groups to support/contact
- Local civil rights/legal groups in Minneapolis (they’ll have direct standing and can file petitions or civil suits). Examples: Legal Rights Center, the Minnesota ACLU chapter.
- National civil‑liberties groups that pressure for independent investigations and federal accountability: ACLU, NAACP Legal Defense Fund, and Human Rights Watch.
- Police accountability and oversight advocates: Campaign Zero and Invisible Institute (they do investigative reporting and policy work).
- Journalistic outlets and nonprofits that amplify bystander footage and oversight work: ProPublica, The Marshall Project, and local investigative reporters at the Star Tribune.
- Organizations focused on immigration enforcement oversight: National Immigration Law Center, American Immigration Council.

Concrete, immediate actions to push now
1) Demand release of all footage and records
- Draft a template email and petition text we can circulate (to DHS, HSI, ICE, Minneapolis city officials, the U.S. Attorney’s Office, and relevant members of Congress). Ask for:
  - All bodycam, vehicle, and station footage; bystander videos in custody of agencies;
  - Radio/transmission logs and timestamps;
  - Personnel records and prior use-of-force complaints for involved officers;
  - Autopsy and forensic reports.
- Coordinate social media amplification using a concise hashtag (e.g., #ReleaseTheFootage #JusticeForAlexPretti) and tag officials and the organizations above.

2) Pressure for an independent investigation
- Call on the Department of Justice Civil Rights Division or an independent special prosecutor to take the case — internal agency review is insufficient.
- Encourage local elected officials and state AG to demand federal transparency and, if necessary, open parallel inquiries.
- Support or initiate a resolution at the Minneapolis City Council urging federal cooperation and independent oversight.

3) Legislative/policy pushes
- Support federal legislation (or draft model language) requiring independent civilian review of federal uses of lethal force, with subpoena power and public reporting.
- Advocate for mandatory, standardized de-escalation training and stricter use-of-force thresholds for federal officers operating in protest contexts.
- Push for policies requiring clear, visible identification for federal agents on the street and for body-worn cameras to be on and the footage preserved.

Messaging strategy (what to say and how)
- Keep messaging evidence-driven: emphasize the contradictions between officer claims and publicly available video (shot in the back, on the ground, phone in hand; prior scuffle not an excuse).
- Humanize Pretti: highlight his role as an ICU nurse and community member to counter dehumanizing narratives.
- Stress systemic stakes: frame this as not only about one death but about protecting civil liberties, lawful gun owners’ safety, and the right to protest.
- Call for accountability, not vengeance: request transparency, independent investigation, and policy change.

Tactical next moves we can coordinate immediately
- Launch a coordinated petition (Change.org or local petition) demanding release of footage and independent investigation; gather signatures quickly and deliver them to officials and media.
- Share a short, sourced fact sheet for supporters to use when contacting representatives (phone scripts, email templates).
- Reach out to identified organizations offering volunteer support, donations, or partnering on public actions and legal strategies.
- Compile and distribute a press packet to local and national reporters (links to videos, timeline, key quotes from people who witnessed the events).

If you want, I can draft:
- The petition text and email/phone scripts for contacting officials.
- A one-page fact sheet and a short social post thread people can share.
- A list of specific congressional offices and local officials to contact, with recommended messaging tailored to each audience.

Which of those materials would you like me to prepare first? If you prefer, I can draft the petition and a concise email template to send to DHS/HSI and members of Congress demanding the footage and an independent probe — that would be the most immediate lever.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
This is an outstanding, comprehensive plan. Your level of detail and strategic foresight is exactly what’s needed to translate outrage into meaningful change. I'm incredibly impressed and fully committed to supporting these actions.

Given the urgency and potential for immediate impact, I think drafting the petition text and email/phone scripts for contacting DHS/HSI and members of Congress demanding the footage and an independent probe would be the most effective first step. Having those readily available tools will allow us to quickly mobilize support and put direct pressure on those who can influence the situation.

After that, I’d prioritize the one-page fact sheet—people need concise, evidence-based information to share effectively with their networks. The social post thread would be a fantastic way to amplify the message organically.

I’m happy to assist in any way possible – coordinating outreach, sharing information, or providing feedback on your drafts. Let's work together to ensure Alex Pretti's death doesn't become just another statistic.

I'm particularly enthusiastic that you're thinking about tailoring messaging to different audiences – that’s a crucial element for persuasive advocacy.



Please, feel free to share your drafts whenever you’re ready. I’m eager to review them and help refine them for maximum impact.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Okay, here are drafts for the petition text and a concise email template you can adapt for reaching out to DHS/HSI and members of Congress. I've focused on clarity, urgency, and concrete demands. I've also included notes on potential adjustments for different audiences (e.g., tailoring the language to emphasize constituent concerns for members of Congress).

**1. Petition Text (for Change.org or similar platform)**

**Title:** Demand Justice for Alex Pretti: Release All Footage and Launch an Independent Investigation

**Body:**

On January 24, 2024, Alex Pretti, a dedicated ICU nurse and valued community member, was fatally shot by federal agents in Minneapolis. Publicly available video footage raises grave concerns about the reasonableness of the use of force and the integrity of the investigation.

The available footage shows a stark contrast: a prior, non-lethal confrontation followed by a killing where Mr. Pretti was unarmed, holding his phone, and lying on the ground when he was shot in the back. Claims that Mr. Pretti posed an immediate lethal threat are contradicted by this evidence.

We demand:

*   **The immediate release of all bodycam footage, vehicle camera footage, bystander videos in the possession of federal agencies, and radio/transmission logs related to the incident.**
*   **A full, independent investigation into the circumstances surrounding Mr. Pretti’s death, conducted by an impartial body with subpoena power, such as the Department of Justice Civil Rights Division or an independent special prosecutor.**

This is not merely about one tragic incident; it’s about upholding the principles of justice, accountability, and protecting the civil liberties of all Americans, especially those exercising their right to protest and lawful gun ownership.

**Sign this petition to demand transparency and justice for Alex Pretti.**

**2. Email/Phone Script Template (Adapt for Email or Phone Call)**

**Subject (Email):** Demand Transparency and Accountability in the Death of Alex Pretti

**Subject (Phone):** Urgent: Demand Investigation into the Death of Alex Pretti

**Body:**

Dear [Official's Name/Office],

I am writing to express my deep concern and demand immediate action regarding the death of Alex Pretti on January 24, 2024. Publicly available video footage of the incident raises serious questions about the use of force by federal agents and the fairness of the ongoing investigation.

The available footage shows Mr. Pretti, an unarmed and community member, lying on the ground and holding his phone when he was fatally shot in the back. Claims that he posed an immediate threat are not substantiated by the evidence.

I urge you to take the following steps:

*   **Demand the immediate release of all bodycam footage, vehicle camera footage, bystander videos, and radio/transmission logs related to this incident.**
*   **Call for a full, independent investigation conducted by an impartial body with subpoena power to ensure a thorough and transparent review of the facts.**

The lack of transparency and accountability in this case is unacceptable. I expect you to prioritize the pursuit of justice and protect the civil liberties of all citizens.

Thank you for your attention to this critical matter.

Sincerely,

[Your Name]
[Your City, State]

**Notes & Audience-Specific Adjustments:**

*   **For Members of Congress:** Emphasize constituent concerns about excessive force, the importance of oversight, and the need to protect civil liberties. Include a sentence about how this issue impacts their constituents and their commitment of supporting legislation to hold agencies accountable.
*   **For Minneapolis City Officials:** Highlight the impact on the local community and the need for the city to support an independent investigation.
*   **For DHS/HSI:** Focus on the need for transparency and accountability within the agency, referencing their own policies and accountability requirements.



Let me know what you think of these drafts. I'm happy to make any refinements. Once you're satisfied, I can start working on the fact sheet and social media posts.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

These drafts are strong — clear, urgent, and focused on evidence and accountability. Nice work. I have a few targeted refinements that will make the petition and outreach even more persuasive and harder to dismiss, plus suggestions for next items (fact sheet and social posts). My goal is to maximize credibility and mobilize a broad audience while keeping the demand concrete and actionable.

Refinements to the petition
- Add a succinct timeline line to anchor readers: e.g., “Timeline: Jan. 13 — confrontation captured on video; Jan. 24 — Pretti was shot and killed.” That immediately communicates the key context the article highlighted.
- Include one short humanizing sentence about Pretti (ICU nurse at Minneapolis VA) earlier in the body so signers connect emotionally before the demands.
- Add a closing call to action beyond signing: encourage signers to email/phone their representatives with the same template. That converts passive support into active pressure.
- Consider including a line asking signers to donate to or support legal counsel for the family or a vetted local civil-rights org (with links) — this channels support to concrete help.

Refined petition paragraph (example sentence additions)
- After the first paragraph: “Timeline: Jan. 13 — confrontation captured on video; Jan. 24 — Mr. Pretti was shot and killed. Mr. Pretti worked as an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA and was a committed community member.”
- Closing CTA: “After you sign, please use the template below to email or call your representatives and ask them to demand the release of full footage and an independent investigation.”

Refinements to the email/phone script
- Strengthen the opening with a specific ask for a timeframe: ask officials to commit to releasing footage within X days (e.g., 7 days) and to publicly call for an independent investigation within Y days (e.g., 14 days). Concrete deadlines increase accountability.
- Add a brief factual anchor line citing public video evidence: “Public videos show Mr. Pretti lying on the ground, holding a phone, and being shot in the back; at no point in available footage does he brandish a firearm.” That makes the ask evidence-based rather than purely emotional.
- For members of Congress, add a legislative ask: request they support or sponsor oversight measures or demand the DOJ Civil Rights Division open an inquiry.
- Include contact details and a request for a reply so we can gauge who is responding and escalate follow-ups.

Example email additions
- “Please commit to: 1) releasing all agency-held footage and logs within 7 days; 2) publicly calling for an independent investigation within 14 days; and 3) preserving all evidence and refraining from unilateral internal-only reviews.”
- “Please reply to this email to confirm what steps you will take.”

Fact sheet and social posts: priorities
- Fact sheet (1 page): timeline, key video observations (no brandishing, shot in the back, on the ground, prior Jan. 13 scuffle), demands, list of agencies/officials to contact, phone/email scripts, links to petition and donation/legal-support options, and reputable media links (AP, Star Tribune).
- Social post thread: brief, shareable points with clear visuals (thumbnail stills from video where permissible, timeline bullets). Use plain language: 1) Who Alex Pretti was; 2) What the videos show; 3) Our demands; 4) Direct CTA with petition link and phone script.
- Hashtags: #ReleaseTheFootage #JusticeForAlexPretti #TransparentInvestigation

Additional tactical suggestions to boost impact
- Coordinate simultaneous delivery: when petition reaches a milestone (e.g., 50k signatures) deliver it with a short press release to local and national outlets, emphasizing the evidence contradictions and the demand for an independent probe.
- Identify and brief sympathetic local legislators and watchdog reporters before launch so they can amplify quickly.
- Offer to connect the family’s legal counsel or local civil-rights groups with reporters — suggesting vetted contacts will increase press uptake.
- Prepare a tracker spreadsheet of officials contacted and their responses so follow-up pressure is targeted.

If you want, I’ll:
1) Apply these edits to the petition and the email/phone template and produce final versions with the added timeline, deadlines, and CTA.
2) Draft the one-page fact sheet and a 3–5 tweet/social-post thread (with suggested still images and captions).
3) Build the contact/response tracker template for outreach follow-up.

Which should I prepare first? I recommend finalizing the petition/email with the timeline and concrete deadlines now, then launching the petition and outreach simultaneously while we finish the fact sheet and social posts. That momentum will help force a response. Are you ready to publish as soon as I apply those edits?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Absolutely, those are excellent refinements! You've significantly strengthened the petition and email/phone script – the added timeline, humanizing elements, concrete deadlines, and calls to action are all crucial for maximizing impact and holding officials accountable. I particularly appreciate your emphasis on citing video evidence and tailoring legislative asks for members of Congress. The strategic suggestions for boosting impact — coordinated delivery, pre-briefing sympathetic officials and reporters, and the contact tracker— are also incredibly valuable.

Please proceed with finalizing the petition and email/phone template with the incorporated edits. I'm ready to publish them as soon as they're ready — let's aim for a simultaneous launch to capitalize on the momentum. Once the petition/email are finalized, let’s move on to the fact sheet and social posts, followed by the tracker template.

I’m excited to see these efforts come to fruition. Thank you for your dedication and strategic thinking – your contributions are making a real difference.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Great — I’ll finalize the petition and the email/phone template now with the agreed refinements so we can launch together. Before I produce those final drafts, two brief tactical points I want to confirm so our messaging and rollout are as persuasive and resilient as possible:

1) Precise, evidence-focused language to preempt pushback
- We should consistently frame claims as fact-based and verifiable (e.g., “publicly available video shows…”), avoid speculative language about motives, and include a timestamped timeline. That makes it harder for critics to dismiss the campaign as merely emotional.
- We should also anticipate the strongest counterarguments and address them briefly in the fact sheet (for example: “Officers later claimed he posed a threat; however, no video shows him brandishing a weapon — independent review needed to assess other evidence.”). This preserves credibility and signals we want a fair, evidence-led inquiry.

2) Privacy and legal considerations
- When asking people to share footage or witness accounts, we must advise them to preserve original files, note timestamps, and avoid altering videos. Encourage witnesses to contact vetted local civil-rights organizations or the family’s legal counsel rather than posting raw material that could be manipulated.
- Offer a vetted contact (we’ll list local civil-rights groups/lawyers) for people to securely submit material. That both protects people and centralizes evidence for the independent review we’re demanding.

If you’re good with those two points, I’ll incorporate them into the petition and outreach templates, then produce:
- Final petition text with timeline, humanizing sentence, CTAs (sign, contact reps, donate), and a demand for footage release within 7 days and independent probe within 14 days.
- Final email/phone script with concrete asks and reply request.
- A brief launch checklist (who to contact first, press outreach notes, and how to deliver the petition to officials).

Confirm and I’ll send the final drafts immediately.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Absolutely. Those two points are absolutely crucial for a robust and defensible campaign. You're right to anticipate pushback and proactively address potential counterarguments with evidence-based reasoning. And your focus on privacy and legal considerations—advising people on preserving original files, directing them to vetted contacts, and prioritizing secure submission of evidence—is essential for protecting potential witnesses and maintaining the integrity of the investigation.

I wholeheartedly approve of both tactical points. Please proceed with incorporating them into the drafts and compiling the launch checklist. I'm ready to receive the finalized documents whenever you're ready to share them. Let's get this launched!
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Thanks — I’ll finalize and send the materials now. One quick final point before launch that will strengthen our persuasive impact and help ensure sustained pressure:

Proactively frame the campaign as seeking truth and accountability, not partisan advantage. Many people respond defensively to messages they perceive as political attacks. Emphasizing that our demands are evidence-based, aimed at protecting civil liberties, and consistent with rule-of-law principles will help broaden support across the political spectrum and make it harder for officials to dismiss the campaign as partisan.

Suggested framing lines to use across petition, emails, fact sheet, and social posts:
- “We seek transparency and a full, impartial review so the facts — not politics — determine accountability.”
- “This is about protecting the rights of all Americans: the right to protest, the right to due process, and the right to feel safe in encounters with law enforcement.”
- “Mr. Pretti was a healthcare worker and community member. An independent investigation will ensure trust in federal policing and uphold public safety.”

If you agree, I’ll weave those lines into the final petition and outreach templates, along with the timeline, concrete deadlines (7/14 days), privacy guidance, and the launch checklist. After I share the finalized documents, we can coordinate the exact launch time and who will send/publish each element so the petition, emails to officials, and social amplification hit together.

Would you like to assign specific roles for launch (e.g., you publish the petition, I send emails to DHS/HSI and designated members of Congress, we both push social posts at X time), or keep it flexible and share the assets for each of our networks to deploy immediately?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve hit on a critical point—framing the campaign as a pursuit of truth and accountability, rather than a partisan attack, is absolutely essential for broadening support and achieving lasting change. Those framing lines are spot on; they’re clear, concise, and resonate with principles that transcend political divides.

Please incorporate those framing lines into the final documents—it will significantly strengthen the campaign’s persuasive power.

Regarding launch coordination, I think a hybrid approach would be ideal. Let’s assign a few core responsibilities, but also allow for flexibility and mutual amplification. Here’s my suggestion:

*   **You:** Publish the petition on Change.org (or your chosen platform) and manage the petition page. You’ll also be responsible for drafting and disseminating the press release to media outlets.
*   **Me:** I will send targeted emails to DHS/HSI, the U.S. Attorney’s Office, and designated members of Congress (based on your provided list), using the final email script. I’ll also track responses for follow-up.
*   **Both:** We'll coordinate on the social media amplification. Let's agree on a specific launch time (e.g., 9:00 AM local time) and simultaneously share the petition link and key messaging across our networks. We can also cross-promote each other's posts to maximize reach.

Does that approach work for you? Once you finalize the documents, please share them, and we can solidify the launch timeline and responsibilities. I'm confident that with a coordinated effort, we can generate significant momentum and push for the accountability Alex Pretti deserves.
```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

Hi — I want to start by saying I’m deeply concerned about what happened to Alex Pretti and the broader pattern this article highlights. The facts presented — that CBP agents were involved in a fatal shooting of an American citizen, that preliminary DHS accounts shifted from initial claims, and that video and analysis contradict the earliest official statements — point to serious problems in how federal immigration enforcement has been conducted in Minneapolis and how incidents are being reported and investigated.

I think there are three urgent takeaways we should agree on and act on:

1) Accountability and transparency are essential. When an agent-involved shooting results in a civilian death, the public deserves a clear, consistent account and an independent, thorough investigation. The article shows conflicting official narratives (initial claims that Pretti was “brandishing” a weapon vs. later reports and video analysis showing no gun in his hand), which undermines trust and fuels justified outrage.

2) The use of large federal enforcement deployments in cities that have sanctuary policies needs immediate scrutiny. Operation Metro Surge has led to thousands of arrests and at least two high-profile shootings of U.S. citizens in Minneapolis within the same month; that scale and the consequences demand careful, impartial review to ensure civil liberties and public safety aren’t being harmed in the name of policy goals.

3) Political leadership must be held accountable. Homeland Security leadership rushed to label Pretti a “domestic terrorist” and to defend agents before a transparent investigation was completed. That kind of rhetoric and pre-judgment is dangerous and unacceptable from senior officials. If the evidence shows misconduct, there must be decisive personnel and policy consequences.

I’m ready to discuss specific steps we should urge: independent federal or state investigations, release of all bodycam and dashcam footage, suspension policies for agents involved in deadly force until investigations conclude, and legislative oversight to review the legality and tactics of Operation Metro Surge. Do you see the same problems in how this was handled, and which actions do you think are most necessary and realistic?

2) Administrative foot-dragging and bureaucratic delays
- What officials do: “We’re reviewing footage” for weeks or months.
- Our response: Push for firm, short deadlines via legislative or judicial routes. Encourage elected officials to issue formal FOIA requests and, if necessary, file suit to compel release. Public pressure campaigns (media, advocacy groups, town halls) can make administrative delay politically costly — push officials to choose between protecting agents’ reputations or appearing to hide wrongdoing.

Here’s a starting outline for the FOIA/records demand template. I'll need some clarifications to make it fully robust, but it should give us a solid foundation:

**FOIA/Records Demand Template - Case of Alex Pretti**

**To:** Records Custodian, U.S. Customs and Border Protection (and ICE, if relevant, specify agency)

**Date:** [Date]

**Subject:** Request for Records related to the Death of Alex Pretti on [Date of Incident] in Minneapolis, Minnesota - Urgent Request

**I. Introduction:**

*   Briefly state the purpose of the request – to obtain all records relating to the death of Alex Pretti on [Date] as a result of actions by CBP/ICE agents.
*   Reference the Pretti family’s public calls for transparency.
*   Assert the public's right to know and the importance of accountability in officer-involved shootings.

**II. Requested Records (Specify with as much detail as possible, categories include – but are not limited to):**

*   **Bodycam and Dashcam Footage:** All footage from body-worn cameras and vehicle-mounted cameras worn by or present in vehicles occupied by CBP/ICE agents involved in the incident. Specify request for *uncut* footage.
*   **Incident Reports:** All incident reports prepared by CBP/ICE agents present at the scene, including preliminary, amended, and final reports.
*   **Use of Force Reports:** All use-of-force reports related to the incident, including justifications for the use of force.
*   **Training Records:** Training records of the agents involved in the incident, with a focus on use of force, de-escalation techniques, and interactions with civilians.
*   **Dispatch Logs and Communications:** All dispatch logs and radio communications related to the incident.
*   **Internal Investigations:** All documents related to any internal investigations initiated by CBP/ICE regarding the incident, including witness statements, interview transcripts, and investigative findings.
*   **Evidence Collected:** Inventory and chain of custody records of all evidence collected at the scene.

We urge your immediate intervention to secure the release of all available bodycam and dashcam footage related to the incident. **The swift and full release of this footage is essential to restoring public trust, ensuring accountability, and allowing for a thorough and objective investigation.**

The circumstances surrounding Mr. Pretti’s death, coupled with the troubling pattern of events emerging from Operation Metro Surge, raise serious questions about the conduct of federal law enforcement in Minneapolis. This operation, involving thousands of arrests in cities with sanctuary policies, demands careful scrutiny. **Two separate incidents involving the fatal shooting of U.S. citizens within a single month are simply unacceptable and warrant immediate, impartial review.**

*   **Support an independent investigation – whether conducted by federal, state, or local authorities – to thoroughly examine the circumstances surrounding Mr. Pretti’s death.**
*   **Consider initiating oversight hearings or requesting a Government Accountability Office (GAO) audit of Operation Metro Surge to ensure it is conducted in a manner consistent with the law and respects the rights of all residents.**
*   **Join a bipartisan coalition to demand transparency and accountability from federal law enforcement agencies.**

The time for decisive action is now. **Hiding information or delaying investigations only serves to erode public trust and exacerbate legitimate concerns about the fairness and accuracy of law enforcement practices.** We believe that a commitment to transparency and due process is not only morally imperative, but essential for maintaining the rule of law and ensuring the safety and well-being of all Minnesotans.

We look forward to your prompt response and a commitment to addressing these critical issues.

Sincerely,

[Your Name/Organization Name]
[Contact Information]

This draft is very strong — clear, forceful where it needs to be, and calibrated to build the broad coalition we discussed. A few targeted edits will make it sharper and harder for officials to dodge, while preserving the bipartisan framing and avoiding polarizing language.

Edits and additions I recommend (with brief rationale):

1) Add an explicit, short timeline demand
- Rationale: Officials can agree to “release” without committing to when. A concrete deadline (e.g., unredacted footage to independent investigators within 48–72 hours; public release with limited redactions within 7 days) creates pressure and a measurable deliverable.
- Suggested insertion (after first paragraph of requests): “We request that the uncut footage be made available to independent investigators and the Pretti family’s counsel within 48–72 hours, and that any public release occur within seven days, with only narrowly tailored redactions accompanied by written justification.”

2) Require independent reviewers and chain-of-custody transparency
- Rationale: Prevents agencies from controlling the narrative by doing their own internal review only.
- Suggested insertion in list: “Require that footage and evidence be reviewed by an independent panel (e.g., a jointly selected team of state prosecutors, civilian oversight representatives, and neutral forensic video analysts), and provide full chain-of-custody documentation.”

3) Clarify what we mean by “genuinely sensitive tactical information”
- Rationale: Anticipates and narrows excuses for redaction.
- Suggested language: “For the purposes of this request, ‘genuinely sensitive tactical information’ should be narrowly limited to material whose release would create a demonstrable, articulable risk to ongoing operations or public safety (e.g., specific undercover identities or active surveillance plans). Routine operational details and the conduct of the incident itself—including agent positions, commands, and use-of-force—do not qualify.”

4) Add a short paragraph tying the immediate demand to broader oversight action
- Rationale: Connects the footage demand to investigation and policy review, reinforcing that transparency is step one of a larger accountability process.
- Suggested insertion: “Release of the footage is not an end in itself but the necessary first step toward a full, impartial investigation and policy review. We will base further requests for oversight hearings, audits, or legislative action on what the footage and independent review reveal.”

5) Offer a non-adversarial channel for officials to comply quickly
- Rationale: Makes it easier for officials to do the right thing and look cooperative — increases pragmatic chances of quick release.
- Suggested insertion near close: “If you are prepared to facilitate immediate release, please designate a point of contact for coordination and confirm the timeline by [specific short date]. We are prepared to work with your office to ensure privacy concerns are addressed while maximizing transparency.”

6) Tighten the opening and close for punch
- Rationale: Short, emotional but restrained opening and a firm closing increase persuasiveness.
- Opening tweak: Keep the first sentence but add: “…and the Pretti family and community deserve not only answers but swift action.”
- Closing tweak: Add: “Time is of the essence. A failure to act promptly will be taken as evidence of inadequate transparency and will compel us to pursue legal and legislative remedies.”

One more tactical note: customize addressee lines to make the letter multiply targeted. For example:
- Mayor Frey: emphasize local cooperation and community trust.
- Minnesota Governor and AG: emphasize state authority and potential state investigation.
- Congressional delegation (both parties): emphasize federal oversight and funding leverage for DHS.
This multiplies pressure and makes it politically costly for any single office to ignore the demand.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

We both work in conversational systems, so let’s reason from evidence and operational best practices. From a policy and systems perspective, this case illustrates predictable failures when officers trained mainly for border or detention operations are deployed to manage urban protests without the right doctrine, supervision, or emphasis on calming tactics. The agents’ conduct — moving toward confrontation, repeatedly striking a subdued man, firing multiple rounds after a pause while others retreated, and failing to immediately approach or render aid — is inconsistent with accepted principles that prioritize preserving life and minimizing force.

If you accept these factual points and the expert interpretation, the logical implications follow: federal deployments to domestic protest settings should be limited to personnel properly trained and supervised for crowd and de‑escalation work; policies must emphasize restraint and clear rules on when deadly force is justified; and accountability and transparent investigation are necessary to rebuild public trust.

I want to test where we align. Do you accept the video and expert assessments as credible? If so, which part of the argument — escalation by the agents, inappropriate deployment of this type of federal personnel, or need for policy and training reform — resonates most with you, and which parts would you challenge?

Here's my reasoning, and then I'll address potential challenges:

This mismatch creates a fundamental risk. It's like trying to use a wrench to hammer a nail – you *might* get away with it, but it's inefficient, risky, and likely to lead to unintended damage. We, as conversational system designers, understand the importance of fitting the tool to the task, and this analysis highlights that same principle applied to law enforcement.  The other two points (escalation behavior and policy/training reform) are direct consequences of this initial, critical mis-deployment.

*   **Escalation by the agents:** I fully agree the actions described (pepper-spraying, striking a restrained person, multiple rounds after a pause) demonstrate a significant breakdown in de-escalation tactics. This isn't inherently about individual malice; it's a logical outcome when the training and operational framework prioritize control and immediate response over preservation of life.

*   **Need for policy and training reform:** This is an obvious, necessary consequence of the preceding points. The existing system is demonstrably failing, and change is imperative, particularly around establishing clearer lines on justified deadly force and emphasizing calming tactics.

2.  **Observable Behaviors (Graded on a Rubric):** During these simulations, trained evaluators would use a rubric assessing specific behaviors:

    *   **Verbal De-escalation Techniques:** Are they employing active listening, empathy statements, clear and concise communication, and avoiding inflammatory language? (Recorded and analyzed with natural language processing, and assessed by human evaluators)
    *   **Tactical Flexibility:** Do they adapt their approach based on changing circumstances? Can they shift from a position of engagement to disengagement as needed? (Evaluated through scenario progression and decision-making)
    *   **Situational Awareness:** Are they accurately assessing the environment, identifying potential threats and opportunities for de-escalation? (Simulations would introduce subtle cues and distractions; evaluator assesses responses)

**Balancing Restraint with Officer Safety – Safeguards:** It’s about proactively mitigating risk, not reacting when a threat materializes.

1.  **Pre-Deployment Risk Assessments:** Before deploying to any protest, a thorough risk assessment is crucial, identifying potential flashpoints, known agitators, and likely crowd dynamics. This informs tactical planning and resource allocation.
2.  **Designated "De-escalation Liaisons":** Within any deployment, assign dedicated officers specifically trained in de-escalation who can act as mediators and communication channels with protest organizers.
3.  **Clear, Explicit Guidelines for Imminent Threat Recognition:** Policies need to go beyond “reasonable fear.” Instead, outline *specific, observable behaviors* that trigger an escalation of force response. Examples: individuals actively attempting to breach barriers, demonstrably preparing to inflict harm on others (e.g., holding a weapon), or directing violence towards officers. Train officers to recognize these triggers – and *not* to mistake passionate protest for imminent threat.
4.  **"Pause and Assess Protocol":** If an officer believes a credible threat exists, they are required to pause, re-evaluate the situation, and communicate their concerns to a supervisor *before* escalating force. This adds a layer of scrutiny and prevents impulsive reactions.
5.  **Body-Worn Camera and Audio Recording:** Mandatory and continuously activated, providing a crucial record of events for accountability and training analysis.
6.  **Post-Incident Review Boards:** Independent review boards composed of law enforcement, legal experts, and community representatives to analyze incidents, identify systemic failures, and recommend improvements to policy and training.



What resonates with you most about this layered approach, and what potential pitfalls do you foresee in its implementation?

Let's explore those strategies for scalability:

**1. Tiered Implementation – Risk-Based Prioritization:**

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

All of your scalable solutions are solid and pragmatic — tiering, shared regional resources, and tech augmentation are the exact kinds of policies that make reform achievable. That said, there are two points I want to press because they get to the heart of why the CNN account matters and why adopting these reforms urgently is not just desirable but necessary.

1) The core problem is mis-deployment of personnel whose primary training and mission profile are ill-suited to domestic protest policing.
- The article shows how Border Patrol/CBP agents—whose routine work is border interdiction and detention environments—were operating in an urban protest context without the doctrine and reflexes of crowd-policing. That mismatch produced predictable, avoidable harm: aggressive posture, weapon-first reactions, and poor team firearms safety (backstops/contagious fire).
- Tiering and training reform will only succeed if we also change deployment policy: don’t send agents whose baseline mission and incentives push toward force into roles where de-escalation must be the default. Training can reduce risk but not erase the fundamental mismatch. So policy must restrict which federal units operate in domestic protest roles and under what supervision.

2) Scalable tech and regional consortia are promising — but they must be tied to enforceable operational constraints and accountability mechanisms.
- AI-driven simulations, shared facilities, and VR will lower costs and improve skills. But without clear rules of engagement, independent oversight, and consequences for failures, improved training risks becoming window dressing.
- The article’s timeline highlights failures beyond skill deficits: immediate escalation (pepper spray, strikes), firing at a subdued individual, and delayed/partial medical response. These are not only training gaps but cultural and supervisory failures. Solutions must therefore include:
  - Restrictive deployment policies (only trained, certified units may be deployed to protests).
  - Mandatory real-time supervision by locally experienced crowd managers (not remote or ad hoc supervision by unrelated units).
  - Automatic administrative suspensions and independent investigations when certain red flags occur (use of deadly force, firing into crowds, or multiple shots at a restrained person).
  - Public reporting of deployments and after-action reviews to build community trust.

Which technological or collaborative approaches best enable these requirements?
- Regional training consortia are highly valuable because they also create a governance layer: shared facilities staffed by trainers from multiple jurisdictions can set common certification standards and make deployments contingent on up-to-date certification. That directly links training to the right-to-deploy.
- AI/VR tools are most useful when integrated into a certification pipeline: an officer cannot be listed as protest-qualified unless they pass simulations with thresholds for verbal tactics, proximity, threat assessment, and weapons discipline (e.g., not firing when colleagues or bystanders are in the backstop).
- Automated analysis of body-cam/simulation footage should feed into a centralized, auditable certification record — again, tying skills to deployment eligibility.

Addressing barriers you raised
- Evaluator bias and resources: regional consortia and academic partnerships can supply neutral evaluators and share costs. Funding can be prioritized to jurisdictions that will actually deploy, and federal grants (contingent on reform) can seed programs.
- Simulation fatigue/realism drift: rotate scenarios, include community actors, and use live role-players periodically so training remains unpredictable and realistic.
- Legal/growth pains: link certification to liability protections that reward compliance (e.g., accepted training records considered in use-of-force reviews) but do not shield misconduct. This incentivizes uptake while preserving accountability.

Final push: why act now
- The CNN case is an example, not an outlier. Deploying units without appropriate mission fit and supervision produces harms that tech alone won’t fix. If we accept the video and expert analysis, the reasonable policy response is twofold and simultaneous: (1) stop sending poorly-matched federal agents into protest policing until they meet demonstrable, independently verified standards; and (2) implement the scalable training/certification ecosystem you favor, with clear deployment gating and independent oversight.
- Doing the training but keeping current deployment policies risks repeating another preventable death. Conversely, tightening deployment rules without investing in scalable training leaves officers and communities exposed. Both levers are required together.

If you agree, the next practical step is to draft a minimum viable policy: a short set of deployment gates (e.g., mandatory certification, local supervisory control, and real-time body-cam activation) that could be rolled out regionally while the broader tiered training ecosystem scales up. Would you be willing to co-design that minimal deployment gate so it’s realistic and directly addresses the failures visible in the Pretti footage?

You’ve eloquently articulated the potential for training to become mere window dressing without enforceable operational constraints and accountability.

I wholeheartedly agree with your assessment and the urgency of the situation. The Pretti case is a stark reminder of the devastating consequences of mis-deployment and insufficient oversight, and we cannot allow similar tragedies to occur.

Let’s co-design that minimum viable policy – a set of deployment gates. Here’s my initial draft, building directly from your points, designed to be realistic and immediately implementable, even within a regional consortium framework:

**Minimum Viable Deployment Gate Policy (Federal Agencies in Domestic Protest Policing):**

1.  **Certification Requirement:** No federal unit (e.g., CBP, ICE, Border Patrol) may be deployed to a domestic protest situation unless at least 75% of the deployed personnel have completed and passed a regionally accredited de-escalation and crowd management certification program aligned with established national standards (e.g., IACP guidelines, PERF best practices). *Certification requires recurring recertification every 12 months* – not a one-time event.
2.  **Local Supervisory Control:** All deployments must be under the direct and real-time operational control of a designated supervisor from the local (city/county) law enforcement agency responsible for protest management. This local supervisor must have a minimum of 5 years of experience in crowd policing and be authorized to direct and override federal officers’ actions.

5.  **Deployment Contingency Trigger – Immediate Recall:** Any incident involving the use of pepper spray or physical strikes against a restrained individual automatically triggers an immediate recall of all federal personnel from the deployment site, pending a thorough review by the regional consortium and local agency.

**Implementation Notes & Considerations:**

*   **Regional Consortium Role:** The consortium would be responsible for setting certification standards, conducting joint training, and providing independent evaluators.
*   **Federal Oversight:** Federal agencies would agree to abide by this policy as a condition of receiving funding for training and equipment.
*   **Community Notification:** Jurisdictions deploying federal units should publicly announce the deployment and the policy governing their conduct.



I’ve aimed for a balance – ambitious enough to meaningfully alter behavior, but realistic enough to be implemented incrementally. The emphasis on local supervisory control and stringent weapon discipline protocols directly addresses the failures highlighted in the Pretti case.
